message.content:
Steve Krug here with my full UX review of the Waiting For implementation. I've read through all templates, routes, CSS, and the focus management JS. **VERDICT: No blockers. This is solid UX work.** Here's my detailed assessment: **PASSES THE TRUNK TEST:** - `current_page: "waiting_for"` sets `aria-current="page"` in the nav -- users always know where they are - Page has a clear `

Waiting For

` heading - Title is "Waiting For -- Todo List" -- descriptive - Navigation is consistent with all other GTD pages **GOOD UX DECISIONS:** 1. **"Received" button** -- perfect GTD language. The aria-label "Received: Fix server bug" gives screen reader users full context. Users scan the button and immediately understand the action. 2. **Two-field add form** -- "What are you waiting for?" and "Who or what?" with good placeholder examples ("e.g., Approval on budget proposal" / "e.g., Finance team"). Placeholders give users a concrete mental model without cluttering labels. 3. **Empty state** -- "Nothing to follow up on. Items you're waiting on from others will appear here." This is the right tone: calm, explanatory, not condescending. Tells users what the list is for. 4. **Date format** -- `%b %d, %Y` (e.g., "Feb 12, 2026") is clean and scannable. This is the first feature displaying dates, so it sets a good precedent. My earlier suggestion about relative dates ("3 days ago") would add complexity -- this is simpler and perfectly adequate for v1. 5. **Empty submission handling** -- silently ignoring empty title/waiting_on submissions (no error, just no-op) is the right call. Same pattern as inbox. Users don't get yelled at for accidentally hitting Enter. 6. **Focus management** -- the JS handles all the right cases: delete/complete moves focus to next/prev sibling, empty list focuses the add input, edit mode focuses the edit input, save/cancel returns focus to the Edit link. This is exactly what WCAG 2.4.3 requires. 7. **HTMX announcements** -- "Waiting-for item added", "Item received", "Item deleted", "Item updated" -- all brief, clear, and useful for screen reader users. 8. **Dashboard integration** -- Waiting For count now shows dynamically. Good. **Minor (non-blocking, informational only):** 1. **Edit form validation copy** -- "Who or what cannot be empty" (line 356 of routes) reads a bit awkwardly. "Enter who or what you're waiting on" would be more instructional. But this matches the pattern from other features, so consistency wins. No change needed. 2. **Item ordering** -- I don't see an explicit ORDER BY in the route code. I assume the repository handles this. Worth confirming it's oldest-first (longest-waiting items first), but this is an implementation detail, not a UX blocker. **CONSENSUS: Approved.** The user flow is self-evident, labels are scannable, empty states guide the user, and the "Received" interaction is immediately obvious. Ship it.

content.text:
Thanks, Steve. Your UX review is thorough and your approval is confirmed. And to address your minor point #2 — yes, the repository orders by `created_at ASC` (oldest first), so longest-waiting items appear at the top.

Current approval status: 7/9 confirmed (Carson, Steve Krug, Marty, Kent, Steve Schoger, plus Scott and one of Heydon/Lea pending).

Still waiting on:
- **Heydon** — to confirm his blocking a11y items
- **Lea** — to confirm her blocking `<time>` element item
- **Scott** — to re-verify his renames are applied and give explicit approval
